subject: FREN
course_description: This course introduces students to the way translation between different literatures, literary genres, and new media impacts our comparative reading of texts. *Note: This course is non-repeatable.
course_notes: (Topic: Pages, Screens, and Floats: The Representation of Black Childhood) (This course aims to examine Black childhood as a critical category of analysis across literary texts, media forms, and cultural narratives in Brazil and the United States. Through comparative and interdisciplinary approaches, students will develop skills in close reading, critical interpretation, and analytical writing while engaging with narratives that address race, history, memory, and cultural belonging.) (The course will be organized into three modules. The first module focuses on literature and literary theory, emphasizing textual analysis and theoretical application. Students will work with the novels Solitária, by Eliana Alves Cruz, and The Nickel Boys, by Colson Whitehead, in conjunction with critical readings from authors such as Robin Bernstein, Wilma King, Betina Love, Carl Suddler, Juliana Teixeira, and Camille Owens. The second module will introduce media analysis through the study of Brazilian and U.S. films and television series. Students will learn to interpret visual narratives, evaluate representational strategies, and assess how media forms shape cultural understandings of Black childhood within specific social and historical contexts. The third module will explore other modes of storytelling, memory, and heritage with particular attention to Carnival and Mardi Gras.) (Throughout the course, students will strengthen their ability to communicate ideas effectively through written and oral work, including short analytical assignments, reading responses, student-led discussions, a research-based paper, and a final creative project that demonstrates sustained critical engagement with the course materials and themes. Main texts: Solitaria -- a novel (by Eliana Alves Cruz) and The Nickel Boys (by Colson Whitehead)
